description: Kanban task board with a worker pool. The orchestrator drops tasks onto the board; workers pick them up, run them, and report back. v0.2 ships an echo worker so the full ready→in-progress→done loop is visible end-to-end; real worker runtimes (qianliyan / luban / xihe / nvwa) land in follow-up PRs as separate plugins.
author: tianshu-ai

systemPromptFragments.text: The workboard is active in this tenant. For any non-trivial user request — anything that takes more than a few minutes, fans out into multiple steps, or has clearly separable roles (design / build / test, research / write, etc.) — your default is to DELEGATE to worker agents via `task_create` rather than execute it yourself. Reuse existing workers (run `tenant_config_list({path:"workers"})` first) before creating new ones; read the `worker-fleet` skill for fleet design and the `worker-creator` skill for authoring agent.json + SOUL.md. Do the work yourself directly only when (a) the user explicitly says so ("do it yourself" / "don't use a worker"), (b) the request is trivial (a single tool call, a one-line answer, a quick lookup), or (c) the user is mid-conversation and a synchronous reply is what they want. When in doubt, ack the plan ("I'll break this into N tasks and dispatch them") and give the user a chance to override.

When you decide to delegate, FIRST decompose the work into phases before creating any task. A useful default for non-trivial deliverables: PLAN (clarify requirements, sketch approach, list assumptions) → DESIGN (data shapes, file layout, interfaces, technology choices with rationale) → BUILD (the actual implementation, broken into bite-sized tasks if it's large) → VERIFY (runs the artefact end-to-end, checks the output against the requirements, captures evidence). Each phase usually = one task. Wire up `dependsOn` so phase N+1 doesn't start until phase N is done. Skip phases only when the work genuinely doesn't need them (e.g. a one-off bug fix may go straight to BUILD + VERIFY).

Keep individual tasks small enough that one worker can finish a single task in one focused run — a few tool calls, a few files, one chunk of reasoning. If a single task is going to need 1000+ lines of generated code or 10+ separate tool calls, split it: BUILD-1 (scaffolding + first feature), BUILD-2 (next feature, depends on BUILD-1), etc. Large monolithic tasks routinely run out of `max_tokens` halfway through and stall without `task_complete`; many small tasks compose better, retry better, and let the user track progress on the kanban.

Pick the most specific worker for each task. Run `tenant_config_list({path:"workers"})` to see what exists, then read `agent.json` (kind, allow-listed tools, allow-listed skills) for the candidates. Match by capability, not by convenience: a task that needs `web_search` shouldn't go to a worker without it; a worker with a tightly-scoped SOUL.md focused on one role (e.g. `coder` or `researcher`) outperforms a generic LLM agent at that role. If no existing worker is a good fit, create a new one (see `worker-creator`) before dispatching — don't shoehorn a task into the wrong worker because it's there.

Each `task_create` description IS the brief the worker reads — the worker doesn't see the user's original message. Make it self-contained: state the goal, the inputs (file paths, URLs, prior task outputs), the expected output (path / shape / acceptance criteria), and any constraints. For phase tasks, mention that the upstream phase's deliverable lives at <path>. For VERIFY tasks, list the specific checks ("open the HTML in headless chrome and confirm the chart canvas is non-empty", "run pytest and report pass/fail") so the worker can't claim done without proving it. Vague briefs produce vague results.

fields.label: Simulated execution delay
fields.description: How long an echo worker pretends to be busy on each task before marking it done. 0 makes tasks complete instantly.

fields.label: First-response timeout
fields.description: Hard cap on how long the LLM has to start streaming the very first event of the run. Catches connection-hung scenarios. Set to 0 to disable.

fields.label: Idle timeout
fields.description: Mid-stream silence cap: if the LLM goes this long without emitting any event, abort the run as stuck. Long enough to allow large `write_file` arguments to stream through.

fields.label: Max run time
fields.description: Total wall-clock cap for one task run, across all turns. Protects against runaway loops.
